cards.text:
To make BaseballPlayer publicly inherit from Person:
{{c1::class BaseballPlayer : public Person}}

cards.front: What two types of things does a child class inherit from the parent class?

cards.question: How many member variables does a BaseballPlayer object have if BaseballPlayer defines m_battingAverage and m_homeRuns, and inherits from Person which has m_name and m_age?
cards.explanation: BaseballPlayer has 2 of its own (m_battingAverage, m_homeRuns) plus 2 inherited from Person (m_name, m_age).

cards.text: When deciding whether to use inheritance, ask: "{{c1::Is a [derived class] a [base class]?}}" If yes, inheritance is appropriate.

cards.back: When a class inherits from another class that itself inherits from another class (e.g., Supervisor inherits from Employee which inherits from Person).

cards.text: In an inheritance chain, {{c1::all}} derived classes inherit functions and variables from {{c1::all}} classes above them in the chain.

cards.question: Will this code compile? Why or why not?
cards.explanation: Base has no access to getValue() which is defined in the derived class BaseballPlayer. Only derived classes have access to base class members, not the other way around.

cards.front: Why is inheritance NOT appropriate when deciding whether to add name/age to BaseballPlayer using composition (making Person a member)?
cards.back: Because "a BaseballPlayer has a Person" doesn't make sense. The correct relationship is "a BaseballPlayer is a Person", which calls for inheritance.

cards.concept: Why inheritance saves work and maintenance
cards.explanation: Inheritance means we don't have to redefine information from the base class. If we update or fix a bug in the base class, all derived classes automatically inherit the changes.
examples: Add new function to Person → Employee, Supervisor, BaseballPlayer all gain access
Add variable to Employee → Supervisor automatically gains access
Fix bug in Person → all derived classes get the fix automatically

cards.text: When a derived class is created, it can access inherited {{c1::public}} and {{c1::protected}} members, but NOT {{c2::private}} members of the base class.

cards.front: Do Employee and BaseballPlayer have a direct relationship if they both inherit from Person?
cards.back: No, they don't have any direct relationship, even though they both inherit from Person. They are sibling classes.

cards.text: By constructing inheritance chains, we create classes that are very {{c1::general}} at the top and become progressively more {{c2::specific}} at each level of inheritance.
